answer:
**Chapter 71 of the *Viṣṇu Smṛti* lays out the duties and proper conduct (*ācāra*) of a *snātaka* (one who has completed Vedic studentship with the ritual bath).**[^1]

This chapter details behavioral norms, prohibitions, attire, and daily practices for a *snātaka*, such as not despising or mocking others; adopting age-appropriate dress, a cotton sacred thread, golden earrings, a bamboo staff, and water-pot; avoiding looking at the rising/setting sun or certain reflections; rules for purification and speech; and emphasizing self-adornment, twilight worship, and Vedic/Smṛti-based righteousness for longevity and prosperity.[^2] It aligns with *snātaka* conduct in other Smṛtis (e.g., avoiding Śūdras for teaching, proper gazing, and bodily discipline), distinguishing it from *brahmacārin* rules in ch. 28 or householder duties in chs. 59–60.[^3]

[^1]: *Viṣṇu Smṛti*, [Chapter 71](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/71/translation/).

[^2]: Key passages include [p-c99cc5]–[p-e41043], e.g., "[p-daf492] Not an arrogant one. // Vi_71.7 //" and "[p-e41043] > That which is declared by the Veda and the Smṛti to be righteous... proper conduct. || Vi_71.90 ||".

[^3]: *Viṣṇu Smṛti*, [Chapter 71](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/71/translation/#p-c99cc5)–[Chapter 72](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/72/translation/) (self-control, *dama*); cf. ch. 28 ([/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/28/translation/#p-622e90]) for *brahmacārin* ending with bath; chs. 59–60 for householders. No direct "snātaka" term in translation, but context matches (searches for "snataka"/"bathed"/"vow end" yielded 0 results).
